true_relatives.note: The broom झाड़ू takes its name from झाड़ (bush/shrub) — a broom was originally a bundle of twigs or shrubby material bound together

useful_coincidences.note: The 'jhār' syllable sounds close to the motion of jarring or shaking dust loose — helps anchor the sweeping action

sound_alikes.note: The first syllable 'jhāṛ' echoes the English word 'jar' — imagine the jarring, sweeping motion of a broom

root.meaning: to shake off, to clear away

evolution.meaning: broom — the tool used for shaking/clearing
to apply the broom; to sweep

root_breakdown.logic: A broom is literally 'the shaker-offer' — and the verb phrase झाड़ू लगाना (to apply/put the broom) is how Hindi expresses the act of sweeping.

etymology_story: The word झाड़ू comes from a Sanskrit root meaning to shake off or clear away — the same root gives झाड़ी (a bush or shrub).
A traditional broom was made by tying together dry twigs or grass, so the tool kept the name of the plant material.
The verb phrase झाड़ू लगाना literally means 'to apply the broom', following the common Hindi pattern of pairing a noun with लगाना to form a verb.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light throughout; झाड़ू has a slightly longer ū at the end

tips.tip: the second word लगाना ends with the same -nā sound as 'banana' — just the last two syllables

memory_hook.primary: A person dragging a bundle of dry twigs bound together across a dusty courtyard floor, leaving a clean stripe behind them
memory_hook.connection: The bundle of twigs visually connects to झाड़ (shrub/twig) — the literal origin of झाड़ू — while the clean stripe shows sweeping in action

examples.hindi: रोज़ सुबह घर में झाड़ू लगाओ।
examples.romanisation: roz subah ghar mẽ jhāṛū lagāo.
examples.english: Sweep the house every morning.
examples.literal: daily morning house in broom apply

examples.hindi: उसने पूरे कमरे में झाड़ू लगाई।
examples.romanisation: usne pūre kamre mẽ jhāṛū lagāī.
examples.english: She swept the entire room.
examples.literal: she-ERG whole room in broom applied

examples.hindi: पहले झाड़ू लगाओ, फिर पोंछा लगाओ।
examples.romanisation: pahle jhāṛū lagāo, phir põchā lagāo.
examples.english: Sweep first, then mop.
examples.literal: first broom apply, then mop apply

examples.hindi: क्या तुमने आज झाड़ू लगाया?
examples.romanisation: kyā tumne āj jhāṛū lagāyā?
examples.english: Did you sweep today?
examples.literal: what you-ERG today broom applied

true_relatives.meaning: to wipe, to clean by wiping
true_relatives.note: Hindi पोंछना (to wipe) and पोंछा (mop) descend from Sanskrit puñch — the wipe/smear root

useful_coincidences.note: पोंछा sounds like 'ponch-a' which rhymes with 'punch' — imagine punching the floor with a wet cloth to mop it

sound_alikes.note: पोंछा sounds very close to 'poncho' — picture someone wearing a wet poncho and dragging it across the floor to mop

evolution.meaning: to wipe, to dry by wiping
the wet cloth or mop used for wiping floors
to apply the mop; to mop the floor

root_breakdown.logic: The root idea is wiping — the noun पोंछा is the tool (a wet cloth), and the verb phrase says 'apply the tool to the floor'.

etymology_story: The root puñch in Sanskrit meant to wipe or sweep away moisture and dirt.
Hindi kept this root in the verb पोंछना (to wipe) and formed the noun पोंछा for the wet cloth or mop used on floors.
The verb phrase पोंछा लगाना follows the same pattern as झाड़ू लगाना — 'to apply the cleaning tool' — and means to mop.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light and even; the first syllable has a nasal ō (põ)

tips.tip: पोंछा sounds almost identical to 'poncho' — same vowels and consonants, almost identical stress

memory_hook.primary: A person wearing a soaking wet poncho, kneeling on the floor and wiping it with the hem of the poncho
memory_hook.connection: पोंछा sounds just like 'poncho', and the wet fabric dragging across the floor visually encodes mopping

examples.hindi: झाड़ू लगाने के बाद पोंछा लगाओ।
examples.romanisation: jhāṛū lagāne ke bād põchā lagāo.
examples.english: After sweeping, mop the floor.
examples.literal: broom applying of after mop apply

examples.hindi: उसने पूरे घर में पोंछा लगाया।
examples.romanisation: usne pūre ghar mẽ põchā lagāyā.
examples.english: She mopped the entire house.
examples.literal: she-ERG whole house in mop applied

examples.hindi: फ़र्श गंदा है, पोंछा लगाना ज़रूरी है।
examples.romanisation: farś gandā hai, põchā lagānā zarūrī hai.
examples.english: The floor is dirty, mopping is necessary.
examples.literal: floor dirty is, mop applying necessary is

examples.hindi: बच्चे ने पानी गिराया, इसलिए पोंछा लगाना पड़ा।
examples.romanisation: bacce ne pānī girāyā, islie põchā lagānā paṛā.
examples.english: The child spilled water, so we had to mop.
examples.literal: child ERG water spilled, therefore mop apply had-to

true_relatives.meaning: a piece of cloth, a rag
true_relatives.note: Hindi कपड़ा comes from Sanskrit karpaṭa, meaning cloth or rag; the same root is distantly echoed in the Hebrew קרפד (a worn fabric term)

useful_coincidences.note: Sanskrit karpaṭa (cloth) and English 'carpet' likely share a common ancestor — Hindi कपड़ा is a fabric cousin of 'carpet'

sound_alikes.note: कपड़ा (cloth) sounds close to 'carpet' — both literally come from the same Sanskrit word for fabric; remembering one gives you the other

root_breakdown.logic: The word simply means a piece of cloth — and in everyday use, कपड़े (clothes) is the most common application, so कपड़े धोना became the standard phrase for doing the laundry.

etymology_story: The word कपड़ा descends from Sanskrit karpaṭa, a general term for cloth or a piece of fabric.
Passing through Prakrit it simplified into kapaḍa and eventually Hindi कपड़ा.
The same Sanskrit root likely also gave rise to the English word 'carpet' via an ancient trade route for textiles.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light on both words; the ṛ in kaṛpe is a retroflex flap

tips.tip: the first word कपड़े sounds very close to 'carpet' — same consonants, just rearranged slightly
the धो in धोना sounds just like English 'dough'

memory_hook.primary: A person pulling a rolled-up carpet out of a washing tub, wringing water out of it with both hands
memory_hook.connection: The carpet image locks in कपड़े (cloth/fabric, related to 'carpet'), and the washing tub encodes धोना (to wash)

examples.hindi: मुझे आज कपड़े धोने हैं।
examples.romanisation: mujhe āj kaṛpe dhone haĩ.
examples.english: I have to wash clothes today.
examples.literal: me-to today clothes washing are

examples.hindi: उसने मशीन में कपड़े धोए।
examples.romanisation: usne maśīn mẽ kaṛpe dhoe.
examples.english: She washed the clothes in the machine.
examples.literal: she-ERG machine in clothes washed

examples.hindi: बारिश के बाद कपड़े धोना मुश्किल है।
examples.romanisation: bāriś ke bād kaṛpe dhonā muśkil hai.
examples.english: It's difficult to wash clothes after the rain.
examples.literal: rain of after clothes washing difficult is

examples.hindi: कपड़े प्रेस करने से पहले अच्छे से धोना।
examples.romanisation: kaṛpe pres karne se pahle acche se dhonā.
examples.english: Wash the clothes well before ironing.
examples.literal: clothes ironing doing from before well with wash

true_relatives.meaning: to push down, to apply pressure
true_relatives.note: Hindi प्रेस is a direct loanword from English 'press' — to iron is literally to press the fabric flat
The English 'press' — and thus Hindi प्रेस — trace back to Latin pressare, to press

sound_alikes.note: प्रेस is literally the English word 'press' — no translation needed, the action is identical

root_breakdown.logic: Ironing is pressing — you push a hot iron down onto fabric to smooth it. The English word captured this perfectly, and Hindi simply borrowed it.

etymology_story: The Latin root premere meant to push or press down — from which English got 'press', 'pressure', and 'impress'.
When electric irons became common, English used the verb 'to press' for ironing clothes because the action is pushing the iron flat onto fabric.
Hindi borrowed the English word directly as प्रेस, and the phrase कपड़े प्रेस करना means precisely 'to press the clothes'.

pronunciation_guide.stress: प्रेस is said exactly like English 'press'

tips.tip: प्रेस is pronounced identically to English 'press' — no adjustment needed

memory_hook.primary: A large red 'PRESS' button on a wall, and below it a neatly ironed shirt hanging on a hanger
memory_hook.connection: The PRESS button directly mirrors प्रेस (the loanword), and the ironed shirt shows the result — pressing clothes flat

examples.hindi: कल मीटिंग है, कपड़े प्रेस करो।
examples.romanisation: kal mīṭing hai, kaṛpe pres karo.
examples.english: There's a meeting tomorrow, iron your clothes.
examples.literal: tomorrow meeting is, clothes press do

examples.hindi: उसने सभी कपड़े प्रेस किए।
examples.romanisation: usne sabhī kaṛpe pres kie.
examples.english: He ironed all the clothes.
examples.literal: he-ERG all clothes pressed did

examples.hindi: कपड़े धोने के बाद प्रेस करना मत भूलना।
examples.romanisation: kaṛpe dhone ke bād pres karnā mat bhūlnā.
examples.english: Don't forget to iron after washing the clothes.
examples.literal: clothes washing of after press doing not forget

examples.hindi: यह कमीज़ बहुत झुर्रीदार है, इसे प्रेस करना होगा।
examples.romanisation: yah kamīz bahut jhurrīdār hai, ise pres karnā hogā.
examples.english: This shirt is very wrinkled, it will have to be ironed.
examples.literal: this shirt very wrinkled is, it-to press doing will-be

true_relatives.meaning: a vessel, a receptacle, something that contains
true_relatives.note: Hindi बर्तन comes from Sanskrit vartana — a container or vessel; the v→b shift is a common Indo-Aryan sound change
Hindi माँजना comes from Sanskrit mārjana, the act of rubbing or polishing clean — the same root gives मार्जार (cat — the self-cleaning animal)

useful_coincidences.note: माँजना sounds faintly like 'manjan' — and scrubbing is exactly what you do at the edges of dirty dishes

sound_alikes.note: माँज (mā̃j) rhymes loosely with 'munch' — imagine munching sounds as you scrub plates hard

root.meaning: to wipe, to rub, to polish, to cleanse

evolution.meaning: to scrub clean — specifically pots and pans

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit root meant to rub and polish until clean — माँजना in Hindi is specifically that vigorous scrubbing motion used on metal pots and dishes.

etymology_story: The verb माँजना descends from Sanskrit mārj, meaning to rub or wipe until clean — the same root that gives mārjāra (cat), the animal that constantly grooms itself.
In Prakrit the word simplified and its meaning narrowed to hard scrubbing rather than gentle wiping.
In Hindi, माँजना is the specific word for scrubbing dishes, pots, and pans — more vigorous than धोना (to wash) and implying scrubbing away burned or crusted food.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light throughout; the ā̃ in mā̃j is a nasalised long vowel

tips.tip: the first syllable 'bar' in बर्तन is identical to the English word 'bar'

memory_hook.primary: A person bent over a large metal pot, scrubbing it so hard with a steel wool pad that sparks fly off the surface
memory_hook.connection: The intense, sparking scrub visually captures the vigour of माँजना — not just washing but aggressively scouring the vessel clean

examples.hindi: खाना खाने के बाद बर्तन माँजना।
examples.romanisation: khānā khāne ke bād bartan mā̃jnā.
examples.english: Scrub the dishes after eating.
examples.literal: food eating of after dishes scrub

examples.hindi: उसने सारे बर्तन साफ़ माँज दिए।
examples.romanisation: usne sāre bartan sāf mā̃j die.
examples.english: She scrubbed all the dishes clean.
examples.literal: she-ERG all dishes clean scrubbed gave

examples.hindi: बर्तन माँजने के लिए साबुन चाहिए।
examples.romanisation: bartan mā̃jne ke lie sābun cāhie.
examples.english: You need soap to scrub the dishes.
examples.literal: dishes scrubbing of for soap needed

examples.hindi: रोज़ बर्तन माँजना बहुत थकाने वाला काम है।
examples.romanisation: roz bartan mā̃jnā bahut thakāne vālā kām hai.
examples.english: Scrubbing dishes every day is a very tiring task.
examples.literal: daily dishes scrubbing very tiring work is
